title: My Conversation with Joanne Paul - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: A Conversations-with-Tyler episode with Tudor historian Joanne Paul, exploring Thomas More's contradictions, fear as the driver of his persecution of heretics, and the alchemical/mystical roots of the scientific revolution.

insights.claim: Thomas More's persecution of heretics is best understood as driven by fear that the Lutheran heresy would cause the breakdown of his society, not by simple villainy.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A historian's framing of how educated people rationalize violence against an out-group through fear of societal collapse — a transferable lens on present-day moral panics.
insights.quote: fear can lead to that hatred and indeed that violence
insights.locator: PAUL: I think that there are important lessons in that for us today

insights.claim: Thomas More personally oversaw only three cases of heretics burned at the stake as Lord Chancellor, contradicting social-media claims that hundreds were executed at his hand.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A concrete corrective on a historical figure whose reputation is distorted online — illustrates how viral history detaches from the documentary record.
insights.quote: There were three cases that he oversaw as Lord Chancellor of those who were burned at the stake.
insights.locator: He imprisoned heretics. He interrogated them.

insights.claim: The scientific method emerged from alchemy and the search for the philosopher's stone, with science and religion/mysticism advancing hand in hand rather than science triumphing over them in the 16th-17th centuries.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Reframes the standard 'science vs. religion' progress narrative — useful for thinking about how today's frontier methods can spring from disreputable or fringe practices.
insights.quote: The methodology for scientific experimentation comes out of this desire to find the philosopher’s stone.
insights.locator: PAUL: A lot of that comes from the Renaissance

insights.claim: The precursors of the scientific revolution in the Tudor period came largely from the Renaissance reintroduction of classical Greek texts and engagement with Greek mathematics and science.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Locates a key innovation wave in the recovery and translation of older knowledge, not new discovery — a pattern relevant to how stagnant fields restart.
insights.locator: There’s this reintroduction of a lot of classical texts

themes.theme: Tudor England as a turning point

insights.claim: The 16th century is historically pivotal because it sits between the medieval and modern worlds, making its 'paths not taken' a way to ask whether the modern world was inevitable.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A counterfactual lens on historical contingency — applicable to questioning whether any current trajectory (tech, institutions) was actually inevitable.
insights.locator: she’s drawn to the 16th century because it sits between the medieval and the modern
